title: Abstract Classes
description: Classes that can never be instantiated directly, forcing subclasses to provide their own implementation of certain methods -- solving the Shape.area() = 0 placeholder from Polymorphism.
objectives: Understand what an abstract class is and why it can't be instantiated
Declare abstract methods that subclasses must implement
Recognize when abstract classes fit better than a plain superclass

sections.text: In Polymorphism, Shape had area() return 0 as a meaningless placeholder, purely so Circle and Square had something to override. But what does a bare Shape's area even mean? Nothing, really -- Shape is a concept that should never exist on its own. Abstract classes express exactly this.

A class declared with the abstract keyword that cannot be instantiated directly (no new AbstractClass() is allowed) and may contain abstract methods -- method signatures with no body, that subclasses are required to implement.

abstract double area(); has NO curly braces, no body -- just a semicolon. This is only legal inside an abstract class. A subclass is REQUIRED to implement area() with an actual body, or it must also be declared abstract itself.

sections.code: // Shape s = new Shape(); // does not compile -- Shape is abstract

sections.text: There is no way to create a bare Shape object -- Java's compiler enforces this directly. This matches the real-world intent: an abstract Shape concept alone doesn't have a computable area; only a concrete shape like Circle or Square does.

sections.title: What's happening here
points: Circle extends Shape and provides a real body for area() -- required, since Shape declared it abstract
printInfo() lives in Shape itself, fully implemented, and Circle inherits it unchanged
printInfo() calls area() -- and thanks to polymorphism (previous lesson), it correctly runs Circle's version, even though printInfo() was written in Shape before Circle even existed

sections.text: An abstract class can freely mix abstract methods (no body, must be overridden) with regular, fully-implemented methods (like printInfo() above) that subclasses simply inherit as-is. This lets a superclass provide shared behavior while still forcing subclasses to fill in the parts that genuinely differ.
6. When to Use an Abstract Class

sections.title: Guideline
points: The superclass represents a concept that should never exist as a standalone object (a generic 'Shape' or 'Animal')
You want to force every subclass to provide its own version of specific behavior (every shape MUST define its own area())
You also have some genuinely shared, ready-to-use behavior to give subclasses for free (like printInfo())

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to create an object directly from an abstract class -- new Shape() never compiles, regardless of what Shape contains.
Giving an abstract method a body -- abstract double area() { return 0; } is invalid; abstract methods must end with a semicolon and no braces at all.
Extending an abstract class without implementing all its abstract methods, and forgetting the subclass itself must then also be declared abstract.
Confusing 'abstract method with no body' with an ordinary method that merely happens to return a placeholder value like 0 -- these are very different things.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is an abstract class?
questions.answer: A class declared with abstract that cannot be instantiated directly and may contain abstract methods -- method signatures with no body -- that any concrete subclass must implement.

questions.question: Can you create an object directly from an abstract class?
questions.answer: No. Java's compiler prevents new AbstractClassName() entirely, regardless of what the class contains.

questions.question: What happens if a subclass doesn't implement all of an abstract superclass's abstract methods?
questions.answer: That subclass must itself be declared abstract as well; only a class that implements every inherited abstract method can be instantiated with new.

questions.question: Can an abstract class contain fully-implemented (non-abstract) methods?
questions.answer: Yes. Abstract classes commonly mix abstract methods (forcing subclasses to implement specific behavior) with regular methods that provide shared, ready-to-use behavior inherited as-is.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: An abstract class, declared with abstract, can never be instantiated directly -- there's no new AbstractClass(). It can declare abstract methods, which have a signature but no body, forcing every concrete subclass to provide its own implementation, while still mixing in regular, fully-implemented methods that subclasses inherit for free. This fits situations like Shape: the concept alone has no meaningful area(), but Circle and Square, as concrete subclasses, must each define their own. If a subclass doesn't implement every inherited abstract method, it must also be declared abstract itself.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: abstract class = an incomplete blueprint -- you can't build a house from it directly. abstract method = a blank the blueprint leaves for each specific subclass to fill in themselves.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Can you instantiate an abstract class directly?
What must an abstract method's body look like?
What happens if a subclass doesn't implement all inherited abstract methods?
Can an abstract class have regular, fully-implemented methods too?
